title: Information Gain: Choosing the Best Split
concept: Information gain measures the reduction in entropy or Gini impurity after a dataset is split on a feature — the split that yields the largest drop in disorder is chosen.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the range of Gini impurity?
exercises.explain: Correct! Gini impurity maxes out at 0.5.

cards.title: Searching for the best question
cards.body: If you want to identify an animal, which question is better: 'Does it have cells?' (almost always yes, doesn't help separate) or 'Does it bark?' (immediately separates dogs from birds). A decision tree wants to ask the most useful questions first. We measure this usefulness using information gain.

cards.title: Information Gain: Measuring the drop in messiness
cards.body: Information Gain is the difference between the entropy *before* a split and the weighted entropy *after* the split. The split with the **highest information gain** is selected as the winning split.

cards.title: Sweeping the floor
cards.body: Think of information gain as the amount of dirt you sweep up. Before you sweep, the floor is dirty (high entropy). After one sweep, you've collected most of the dirt into a neat pile. The 'gain' is how much cleaner the floor became after your action.

cards.title: Evaluating splits
cards.body: A tree splits 100 fruits. Question A ('Is it round?') leaves you with two messy groups (entropy drops from 1.0 to 0.95, gain = **0.05**). Question B ('Is it orange?') splits them into pure oranges and pure apples (entropy drops from 1.0 to 0.0, gain = **1.0**). The tree selects Question B.

cards.title: Trees don't plan ahead
cards.body: You might assume a decision tree looks at the whole board and plans a 10-step strategy. In reality, trees are **greedy algorithms**. They only look at the immediate next split and choose whatever gives the highest information gain *right now*, even if a different split might have been better three levels down.

cards.title: How Information Gain Chooses Splits
cards.body: Comparing a low-gain split to a high-gain split:

left.text:
Entropy drops slightly (e.g. 1.0 -> 0.9).
Groups are still mixed.
Poor question.

right.text:
Entropy drops significantly (e.g. 1.0 -> 0.1).
Groups are mostly sorted.
Excellent question.

exercises.prompt: How does a decision tree decide which feature to split on first?

options.text: It selects the feature that provides the highest Information Gain.
It selects a random feature to keep calculations simple.
It selects the feature with the lowest variance threshold.
It selects the last column of the training dataset.

exercises.explain: Correct! The tree calculates information gain for all possible splits and chooses the highest one.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Information gain is calculated by subtracting the entropy *after* a split from the entropy *before* the split.
exercises.explain: True! Information gain measures the difference (reduction) in entropy caused by splitting.

exercises.sentence: Because decision trees select the best split at each step without planning ahead, they are called ___ algorithms.
exercises.explain: Greedy algorithms make the locally optimal choice at each stage, hoping it leads to a global solution.

exercises.prompt: Which of these questions would yield the HIGHEST information gain if we want to classify emails as Work or Personal?

options.text: Does the sender domain end in '@company.com'?
Does the email contain the letter 'e'?
Was the email received on a Tuesday?

exercises.explain: Correct! Email domains ending in @company.com will strongly partition work emails from personal ones, resulting in a large drop in entropy.

exercises.prompt: A dataset of animals has an initial entropy of 1.0. A split on the feature 'Has Feathers' results in groups with a weighted average entropy of 0.2. A split on 'Is Warm-Blooded' results in groups with a weighted average entropy of 0.8. What is the information gain for each split, and which does the tree choose?

options.text: Information gain for Feathers = 0.8; Warm-Blooded = 0.2. The tree splits on 'Has Feathers'.
Information gain for Feathers = 0.2; Warm-Blooded = 0.8. The tree splits on 'Is Warm-Blooded'.
Both splits have information gain of 1.0. The tree chooses randomly.
Information gain cannot be calculated without the number of animals.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Information gain is the drop in entropy: for Feathers it is 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8, and for Warm-Blooded it is 1.0 - 0.8 = 0.2. The tree chooses 'Has Feathers' because it provides the highest information gain. Next, let's trace a decision tree end-to-end in action!
